## Tagline

Ask questions about any codebase — cited answers only.

*Character count: 53/60 ✓*

## Description

RepoBrain indexes your Git repo across semantic, lexical, and structural retrieval — then answers questions with real file:line citations. No hallucinated paths. No guessing. Just sourced answers from your actual code.

*Character count: 199/260 ✓*
*Unique mechanism present (three-strategy retrieval + citation enforcement). ✓*

---

## First Comment (Maker Intro — post immediately after launch)

Three years ago I joined a codebase with 180,000 lines of TypeScript and no documentation. I spent my first two weeks asking the same question in different forms: "where does X happen?" Sometimes a teammate knew. Most of the time I traced it myself — following imports, reading call chains, hoping I got everything. I made two changes in my first month that broke things I didn't know were connected.

The problem wasn't complexity. It was opacity. The codebase couldn't explain itself.

RepoBrain is my answer to that. It indexes a Git repo across three retrieval strategies — semantic (what does this mean?), lexical (what words appear?), structural (what calls what?) — and answers questions with citations that point to real file:line ranges in your code. The AI is instructed to only use what retrieval returned. That's why the citations are real, not hallucinated. You can click any one and land on the exact line.

Beyond chat, there are 15 features built on the same index: blast radius analysis, natural language structural search ("find error handlers without retry logic" actually works), architecture diagrams that update on re-index, specialist agents for code review and security audits, a Git timeline that shows features emerging across commits, and more.

I'm especially looking for: senior engineers and tech leads who regularly navigate codebases they didn't write, and teams who've felt the friction of "LLM answers about code but hallucinates the paths." If that's you, I'd genuinely like to know if RepoBrain's cited answers change the experience.

Index your first repo free at repobrain.dev — founding price ($29/mo Pro) is live until Friday, May 17th.

*Word count: 228 words ✓ (target: 150–250)*

## Maker Comment (Response template — for replying to upvoters)

Thank you — that means a lot. Quick question: what's the codebase question you dread most — the one you always end up tracing manually because nothing else gives you a reliable answer? I want to know if RepoBrain handles it well, or if it's an edge case we should prioritize.

**Variant — for engineers who mention Copilot or ChatGPT:**
Thank you! Curious: have you tried asking Copilot Chat something where it gave you a plausible-sounding answer with a wrong file path? That's the specific problem RepoBrain was built to fix. Would love to know if you've hit that and whether the citation-grounded approach solves it for you.

**Variant — for founders/PMs (non-engineers):**
Thank you! Honest question: how much time does your engineering team spend in "understanding before acting" mode — reading code before making a change? We're trying to quantify this better and any data point helps.
